text: The surgeon opens the lesser sac by incising the gastrocolic omentum, revealing the underlying structures necessary for the pancreaticoduodenectomy.
The surgeon mobilizes the colon up to the hepatic flexure, providing complete exposure of the duodenal C-loop.
The duodenum is fully exposed, revealing its anatomical structure and surrounding tissues.
The surgeon dissects the right gastroepiploic artery and removes the lymph nodes along this artery below the pylorus, directing them towards the pancreas as part of the regional lymph node packet.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to dissect the posterior aspect of the first part of the duodenum, with the pylorus and duodenum visibly exposed.
The surgeon identifies the communicating branch between the middle colic vein and the right gastroepiploic vein, highlighting its potential for heavy bleeding if accidentally torn.
The surgeon exposes the inferior pancreaticoduodenal vein and carefully ligates it, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses the butt of the robotic scissors to tunnel around the superior mesenteric vein, while lifting the pancreas with robotic forceps to facilitate the dissection.

The surgeon carefully tunnels around the anterior aspect of the superior mesenteric vein, ensuring no injury to branches that drain into the vein, which are typically absent in this area, making the procedure uneventful.
The surgeon performs complete caucarization of the duodenum, approaching from below upwards, using robotic instruments to facilitate precise dissection.
The surgeon dissects along the ligament of Treitz, delivering the jejunum into the supracolic compartment, with visible manipulation of the intestinal structures.
The surgeon transects the jejunum at a sufficient length, ensuring adequate tissue separation for the procedure.
The surgeon clears lymph nodal structures in the porta hepatis, exposing and carefully dissecting around the left hepatic artery.

The surgeon identifies, clips, and cuts the right gastric artery, followed by the gastroduodenal artery. The portal vein is visualized between the common hepatic duct and the common hepatic artery. The common hepatic duct is being dissected.
The surgeon secures the common hepatic duct with an endo-bulldog clamp and cuts it at a sufficient length, preventing bile spillage.
The surgeon completes the tunneling around the superior mesenteric vein and passes a tape through the created tunnel, indicating the vein's isolation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon transects the first part of the duodenum using a stapler while preserving the pylorus. The pancreas is also transected.
The surgeon performs a cholecystectomy, removing the gallbladder as one of the final steps of the pancreaticoduodenectomy procedure.

The surgeon carefully dissects the uncinate process, a challenging and vascular area, using the third robotic arm to retract the vein. Below the vein, the superior mesenteric artery is exposed, requiring careful handling to avoid injury. The superior pancreaticoduodenal vein, draining into the portal vein, is secured with clips and cut, completing the dissection of the uncinate process.

The surgeon cannulates the small pancreatic duct using a six French infant feeding tube. A pancreaticojejunostomy is performed with a 4-0 BDS suture, starting with the posterior layer as a continuous suture.

The surgeon performs a jejunostomy, placing interrupted sutures between the pancreatic duct and the jejunostomy site. An infant feeding tube is used as a stent and left in situ, completing the ductomucosa anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the fourth layer of the anastomosis using PDS 4-0 suture material. The bulldog clamp over the bile duct is released, and an anastomosis is formed between the jejunum and the bile duct with interrupted sutures.
The surgeon performs a gastro-jejunostomy, creating an anastomosis between the stomach and the jejunum.
